169.reason: [Assumes escape sequence is always 4 characters (e.g., '\n'), but doesn't check bounds or handle invalid escapes; also fails if string ends prematurely]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [Assumes there are always 4 characters for an escaped char literal (e.g., '\n'), but doesn't check bounds; may cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException or accept invalid literals like unterminated ones]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.2 , reason:  [Assumes there are always 4 characters for an escaped char literal (e.g., '\n'), but doesn't check bounds; may cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException or incorrect parsing if input ends early or has malformed escape]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Assumes there are always 4 characters for an escaped char literal (e.g., '\n'), but doesn't check bounds; may cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException or accept invalid literals like unterminated ones]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [does not check bounds; may cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException if input ends prematurely, but more critically, assumes all escape sequences are exactly 2 chars (e.g., '\n'), but substring(i,i+4) takes 4 chars: e.g., '\'' + '\\' + 'n' + '\'' → correct length is 4, but if input is incomplete like "'\\", this fails. However, the main lexical error issue is elsewhere.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Assumes every char literal has exactly 3 characters like 'a', but doesn't validate if the closing quote exists; may read beyond string length or accept malformed literals like 'a without closing quote]

190.reason: [Only consumes one '&' and moves on, but does not record the erroneous single '&' as a token or skip it properly in all contexts; however, main issue is below]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [Only consumes one '&' and moves to next character, but does not skip the rest of the token or handle possible following valid tokens correctly; however, main issue is below]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.2 , reason:  [Only increments i by 1 after detecting single '&', but should skip the '&' itself; however, since word was set to "&" and not added due to error, this may be okay—but more critically, it does not consume the next character if it's not '&', leading to potential reprocessing or missing error context]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Only increments i by 1 after detecting a single '&', but does not consume the '&' as a token or skip further processing; however, more critically, it fails to check if i+1 is within bounds before accessing content.charAt(i+1), which can cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException at end of file]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [after detecting single '&', only increments i by 1, leaving the next character (which was checked as content.charAt(i+1)) to be processed again in the next loop iteration, potentially causing mis-scanning or double-processing. More critically, it does NOT skip the '&' itself properly—it adds error but then continues; however, the bigger issue is that after setting existError=true, the lexer still continues processing and may add more tokens or errors, but the spec says each erroneous example has exactly one error and should output only that. But the core bug for error type 'a' is here.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-4: 0.2 , reason:  [Same issue: accesses content.charAt(i+1) without checking if i+1 < content.length(), risking StringIndexOutOfBoundsException when '&' or '|' appears at the last character of input]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-5: 0.2 , reason:  [same issue as with '&': after detecting single '|', only increments i by 1, so the '|' is consumed (i moves past it), but the logic is correct in that it reports error and moves on. However, note that in both '&' and '|' cases, after reporting error, the code does NOT add any token for the invalid symbol, which is correct. BUT: the real critical flaw is that after setting existError = true, the loop continues processing the rest of the file, which violates the requirement that the program should stop or only report the first error? However, the problem states: "each erroneous example will have exactly one error", so multiple errors won't occur, but the main issue is: when a single '&' is found, it reports error, increments i by 1 (so now i points to the next char after '&'), and then proceeds. That seems okay. However, look closely: what if the single '&' is at the end of file? content.charAt(i+1) would throw StringIndexOutOfBoundsException. But test case shows compilation error due to javac not found, which is misleading. The actual bug is likely in the handling of single '&' or '|'. But wait: the error type 'a' is correctly reported. So why failure?]

214.reason: [Correctly handles single-char operators, but again, accesses content.charAt(i+1) without checking if i+1 < content.length(), which can cause runtime exception on last character]
